name: Shikome
description: Shikome are fearsome female demons appearing in Japanese mythology, particularly in the creation myths documented in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki. They are described as monstrous beings, often depicted with grotesque features, sometimes with animalistic traits, and embodying primal chaos. Shikome are associated with the tumultuous era before the establishment of order in the world, representing the untamed and dangerous aspects of the natural world. Their existence highlights the struggles of the deities in establishing civilization and the constant threat of the primordial forces seeking to disrupt the established order. They served as antagonists to the early gods, embodying resistance to the divine will. The Shikome's defeat and subjugation is a crucial step in the cosmogony, marking the transition from chaos to cosmos, solidifying the power of the ruling deities and establishing the foundation for Japanese society and culture. They are considered harbingers of misfortune and are symbolic representations of the dangers lurking in the untamed wilderness.
subtitle: Grotesque female demons of Japanese myth.
appearance: Shikome are often depicted as monstrous women with grotesque features, wild hair, sharp claws, and sometimes animalistic characteristics like horns or fangs. Their appearance is generally frightening and unsettling, reflecting their chaotic nature.
habitat: Wild, untamed areas, such as mountains, forests, and caves, often associated with places of spiritual power or danger.
behavior: Shikome are typically depicted as malevolent and aggressive, seeking to disrupt order and cause harm to humans and gods alike. They are driven by primal instincts and a desire to return the world to a state of chaos. They may attack travelers, spread disease, or attempt to corrupt the righteous.

keyMyths.title: The Descent of Ninigi
keyMyths.description: When Ninigi-no-Mikoto, grandson of Amaterasu, descended from the heavens to rule Japan, he was initially met with resistance by various earth deities, including the Shikome. Their chaotic presence threatened his divine mission, symbolizing the challenges he faced in establishing order and civilization.

keyMyths.title: The Subjugation of the Earth Deities
keyMyths.description: In many accounts of early Japanese mythology, the Shikome are depicted as part of a larger group of earth deities who opposed the celestial deities. The subjugation of these earth deities, including the Shikome, represents the triumph of order over chaos and the establishment of the divine rule on earth.

keyMyths.title: Omizunu and the subjugation of earth spirits
keyMyths.description: Omizunu, son of Ōkuninushi, is said to have subdued earth spirits, including Shikome, in order to establish order in Izumo. This story highlights the ongoing struggle between the divine forces and the unruly spirits of the land, paving the way for the establishment of a stable society.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: A complete translation of the Kojiki with detailed annotations, providing valuable insights into the creation myths and the roles of various deities and creatures, including the Shikome.
sources.text: A complete translation of the Kojiki with detailed annotations, providing valuable insights into the creation myths and the roles of various deities and creatures, including the Shikome.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: A comprehensive translation of the Nihon Shoki, another essential source for understanding Japanese mythology and history, offering alternative perspectives on the Shikome and their significance.
sources.text: A comprehensive translation of the Nihon Shoki, another essential source for understanding Japanese mythology and history, offering alternative perspectives on the Shikome and their significance.

sources.author: Carmen Blacker
sources.work: The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan
sources.description: Explores the broader context of spiritual beings in Japanese folklore and religious practices, offering insights into the cultural significance of creatures like the Shikome within the framework of shamanism and animism.
sources.text: Explores the broader context of spiritual beings in Japanese folklore and religious practices, offering insights into the cultural significance of creatures like the Shikome within the framework of shamanism and animism.
